metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly in the What section (or as an AC) that the gate step must ensure `origin/main` is fetchable — e.g. add a `git fetch origin main --depth=1` before the lock comparison, or require `fetch-depth: 0` in the checkout step of both workflow files.
evidence: Plan states: 'compare against origin/main's limit and fail with an 'must override' ::error:: if it differs'
No mention of fetch-depth configuration or a prerequisite git-fetch step in either workflow.
GitHub Actions `actions/checkout` defaults to `fetch-depth: 1` (shallow clone), which may not expose `origin/main` as a ref for file-reading via `git show origin/main:.github/module-size-limit.txt`.
findings.finding: The limit-lock step compares against `origin/main`'s limit file, but the plan does not specify whether the CI checkout is configured to make `origin/main` available (e.g. fetch-depth > 1 or an explicit `git fetch origin main` prior step), risking a silent failure or git error on shallow clones.
findings.location: What section, item 2(c): 'LIMIT LOCK — compare against origin/main's limit'
scenarios: On a shallow-clone CI checkout, `git show origin/main:.github/module-size-limit.txt` returns an error; the gate's bootstrap-case logic ('origin/main has no limit file yet, so it does not fire') may be silently triggered even after main has the file, masking the lock.
An executor adds the gate shell without the fetch prerequisite; the lock never fires, defeating the admin-lock mechanism.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to What section item 2(c): 'Before the lock comparison, run `git fetch origin main --depth=1` (or confirm the checkout step sets `fetch-depth: 0`); document this as a prerequisite in both workflow files.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC verifying the bootstrap path: when `origin/main` has no `module-size-limit.txt`, the lock check exits 0 (e.g., `BASE=$(git show origin/main:.github/module-size-limit.txt 2>/dev/null || echo '') && [[ -z $BASE || $BASE -eq $LIMIT ]] → exit 0`).
evidence: Plan text: 'LIMIT LOCK — compare against origin/main's limit and fail with an 'must override' ::error:: if it differs (bootstrap: origin/main has no limit file yet, so it does not fire on THIS change)'
The five ACs covering the lock test only: message presence, simulated BASE=800/LIMIT=900 → exit 1, simulated BASE=800/LIMIT=800 → exit 0. No AC covers BASE=absent/LIMIT=800 → exit 0.
E3 criterion: 'each non-deferred goal has a faithful, end-state-observable proof'
findings.finding: The bootstrap behavior — 'origin/main has no limit file yet, so [the lock] does not fire on THIS change' — is a non-deferred behavioral claim that affects this change's own CI run, but no AC verifies the bootstrap path (BASE=absent, LIMIT=800 → exit 0). If the implementation instead exits 1 when origin/main's limit file is missing, this change's CI will fail with no observable proof in the ACs to catch that regression.
findings.location: What §2(c) — limit-lock bootstrap note; Acceptance Criteria (none cover the bootstrap path)
scenarios: A developer implementing the lock step treats a missing origin/main limit file as an error (exit 1) rather than a skip — the change's own CI gate fails on submission, but no AC exists to distinguish this bug from a passing implementation before the PR is merged.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'The lock silently skips when origin/main has no limit file (verify: simulate absent BASE — e.g., `BASE= LIMIT=800 <lock-snippet>` → exit 0).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies the gate FAILS (exit 1) when a `src/rebar/**/*.py` file exceeds the limit (e.g., synthesize a file with 801 lines and run the gate step → exit 1).
evidence: 'ABSOLUTE cap — fail if any src/rebar/**/*.py exceeds $LIMIT (no allowlist, no exemptions)'
AC #6 says 'asserts no src file over the file-sourced cap and passes' — tests only that current files pass
ABSENCE: no AC says 'run the gate against a tree with a file >800 LOC → exit 1'
findings.finding: The absolute-cap rejection path — gate fails when a `src/rebar/**/*.py` file exceeds $LIMIT (What item 2b) — has no covering AC that exercises the failure mode; only the passing path is tested by the contract test.
findings.location: What, item 2(b); Acceptance Criteria section
scenarios: The gate shell is implemented with an off-by-one (≥ vs >) or wrong glob and silently accepts a 901-LOC file; no AC would catch it
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '- [ ] The gate rejects an over-cap file (verify: create a temp `src/rebar/test_big.py` with 801 lines, run the gate step → exit 1 with a file-too-large message; then remove the temp file)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (crafted-tree shell snippet or pytest parametrize) that runs the gate against a synthetic tree containing a file with $LIMIT+1 lines and asserts exit 1, proving the cap-exceeded branch actually fires.
evidence: AC line 2 verifies the gate reads the right files but not that it rejects an oversized file
'assert no src/rebar file exceeds it' in contract test is a current-state tautology if all files are already under cap
Notes say 'oracle = running it against crafted trees' but no AC formalizes the oversized-file failure tree
The missing-file guard (AC line 4) and limit-lock (AC line 5) have crafted-tree ACs; the cap-exceeded path does not
findings.finding: The plan has no AC (automated or crafted-tree) that exercises the core enforcement failure path: a source file exceeding $LIMIT causes the new gate to exit 1. The contract test only asserts the current repo is clean (a state assertion); the workflow greps only confirm allowlist refs are absent. Neither proves the gate shell logic would actually fire on an oversized file.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — no AC covers 'gate fails when a src/rebar/**/*.py file exceeds the limit'
scenarios: The gate shell logic has a bug in the size-comparison branch (e.g., wrong comparator or variable name); all ACs pass green because no file is currently over 800 LOC, so the broken branch is never exercised
A future refactor silently drops the cap check; the contract test still passes because no current file is over cap
findings.suggested_fix:
Add an AC after the current AC line 2:
```
- [ ] Gate rejects an oversized file (verify: create a temp file with 801 lines, run the gate step against that tree → exit 1; then remove the temp file → exit 0)
```
Or in `test_module_size_contract.py`, add a parametrized test that monkeypatches the glob result with a fake path whose `wc -l` returns LIMIT+1 and asserts the contract would flag it.

findings.finding: The plan's ACs include simulated tests for the missing-file guard (AC4) and limit-lock (AC5) by running crafted shell snippets, but NO AC simulates the core absolute-cap enforcement path — i.e., running the gate against a crafted file tree containing a `.py` file with >800 lines to assert exit 1. All ACs that touch the cap either do a structural grep (AC2: limit file referenced) or verify actual repo files are already under 800 (AC6/pytest), so a bug in the `wc -l` comparison logic would go undetected.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2, AC4, AC5, AC6 (no AC covers 'oversized file → gate exits 1')
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'Oversized-file cap test: create a temp dir with src/rebar/big.py containing 801 lines, set LIMIT=800, run the gate step → assert exit 1 and output names the offending file.' Mirror the AC4 approach (extract step, craft inputs, assert exit code).

findings.finding: No acceptance criterion exercises the shell gate's absolute-cap enforcement end-to-end (running the actual shell step against a tree that contains an over-cap file). The Notes section acknowledges 'E5: the gate is shell (oracle = running it against crafted trees, per the ACs)' but the ACs only grep for strings in the workflow files or run Python tests — neither actually executes the shell gate against a crafted tree.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — all AC bullets; Notes section ('E5: the gate is shell (oracle = running it against crafted trees, per the ACs)')
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'Simulate cap enforcement: create a temp file of 801 lines; run the gate snippet `LIMIT=800; wc -l <file> | awk ...`; assert exit 1. Create a file of 800 lines; assert exit 0.' This can be a small shell script or an additional pytest parametrized case in the contract test.

findings.finding: The absolute-cap failure path of the new gate script (a file with wc -l > $LIMIT causing an exit 1) has no behavioral acceptance criterion that actually runs the gate against a crafted over-cap tree. The Notes state the oracle is 'running it against crafted trees, per the ACs' but no AC exercises this path; AC2 only greps for the limit-file reference structurally, and AC4 tests the contract on the real codebase (all files already under cap).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 / Notes (E5 sentence)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: 'The gate rejects an over-cap file: create a temp file with 801 lines under src/rebar/, run the gate step (or the relevant shell snippet), assert exit 1; a 800-line file passes (exit 0). Verify: `python -c "open('/tmp/src/rebar/big.py','w').write('x\n'*801)"` then run the cap-check snippet.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (or specify in the plan) that both workflow gate steps ensure `origin/main` is a resolvable local git ref before the limit-lock comparison runs — e.g., by adding an explicit `git fetch origin main --depth=1` within the gate step or confirming the checkout action leaves `origin/main` accessible.
evidence: Plan text: 'compare against origin/main's limit and fail with an "must override" ::error:: if it differs'
Plan text: 'bootstrap: origin/main has no limit file yet, so it does not fire on THIS change'
gerrit-verify.yaml uses `checkout-gerrit-change-action` (SHA-pinned shallow patchset fetch); no `git fetch origin main` step exists in the workflow
test.yml uses `actions/checkout@v7` (default shallow clone of the triggering ref, not necessarily fetching main)
Neither workflow currently uses `origin/main` in any gate step (confirmed by search for 'origin/main' and 'fetch origin' returning no matches)
The plan does not specify that the gate step must include a `git fetch origin main --depth=1` before the comparison
findings.finding: The LIMIT LOCK mechanism relies on reading `origin/main`'s limit file (to detect if the limit changed vs. main), but neither workflow is verified to have `origin/main` available as a local git ref at the point the gate step runs. In `gerrit-verify.yaml` the checkout action (`checkout-gerrit-change-action`) performs a shallow fetch of the patchset refspec only — `origin/main` may not exist in the local repo. The plan acknowledges the bootstrap case ('origin/main has no limit file yet') but does not address whether `origin/main` will be fetchable once the file lands on main. If the ref is absent, the lock silently skips or errors, defeating the admin-lock guarantee.
findings.location: What / item 2(c): 'LIMIT LOCK — compare against origin/main's limit'
scenarios: A contributor submits a change that raises 800→900 in module-size-limit.txt; in gerrit-verify.yaml, origin/main is absent so the lock comparison either silently passes (if the shell script guards with `|| true`) or errors out with a confusing git error — neither is the intended 'must override' enforcement.
In test.yml on a PR branch, actions/checkout may fetch only the PR head, not main's tip, so BASE reads as empty and the lock never fires even when the limit is changed.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a fetch sub-step inside the gate's shell script before the lock comparison: `git fetch origin main --depth=1 2>/dev/null || true` and then read the base limit via `git show origin/main:.github/module-size-limit.txt 2>/dev/null`. Explicitly document this fetch in both workflow files' gate step comments.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify (and verify) the exact shell command used to read `origin/main:.github/module-size-limit.txt` in BOTH workflows, and add an explicit `git fetch origin main --depth=1` step (or equivalent) before the Module-size gate step in gerrit-verify.yaml to guarantee `origin/main` is resolvable in the `checkout-gerrit-change-action` environment.
evidence: Plan states: 'compare against origin/main's limit and fail with an "must override" ::error:: if it differs'

gerrit-verify.yaml uses `lfreleng-actions/checkout-gerrit-change-action@f87b90a3370e62dad310797fedc7fd3700c75832` (checks out a refs/changes/* refspec) — no fetch-depth:0 or explicit `git fetch origin main` in any existing step
test.yml uses `actions/checkout@v7` with no fetch-depth override — shallow clone; origin/main availability on PR vs push triggers differs subtly
No existing usage of `git show origin/main:<path>` or equivalent in either workflow file (search found origin/main only in release.yml where fetch-depth:0 is explicit)
Evidence class for gerrit-verify.yaml: Unverified; Evidence class for test.yml: Partially-verified
findings.finding: The limit-lock mechanism (What §2c: 'compare against origin/main's limit') asserts that `origin/main` is a resolvable git ref in the gerrit-verify.yaml runner context, but this is unverified: `checkout-gerrit-change-action` fetches a Gerrit `refs/changes/*` refspec and it is not established whether `origin/main` is available without an explicit `git fetch origin main` step. The plan specifies the lock behavior but not the shell plumbing to make the ref accessible, creating a capability gap that would cause the lock to silently fail or error at runtime in the Gerrit workflow.
findings.location: What section, item 2(c): 'LIMIT LOCK — compare against origin/main's limit'
scenarios: On gerrit-verify.yaml: the gate step runs `BASE=$(git show origin/main:.github/module-size-limit.txt 2>/dev/null)` but origin/main is not fetched by checkout-gerrit-change-action, so the command returns empty/errors and the lock silently never fires (or erroneously fails the step).
On test.yml push-to-main: the checkout may not set up origin/main pointing to the prior commit, so the lock compares LIMIT against itself (same commit) and always passes.
findings.suggested_fix: Before the Module-size gate step in both workflows, add a fetch step: `git fetch origin main --depth=1 2>/dev/null || true`, then in the lock snippet use `BASE=$(git show origin/main:.github/module-size-limit.txt 2>/dev/null || echo '')` and treat an empty BASE as the bootstrap case (skip lock). Document this in the plan's What §2(c).

findings.finding: Step 2a specifies 'fail if the file is missing OR not a positive int' but no acceptance criterion maps to the 'not a positive int' branch — its error message and exit behavior are unverified.
findings.location: What — Step 2a; Acceptance Criteria (no AC covers the non-positive-int validation path)
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to ACs:
```
- [ ] The gate FAILS when the limit file contains a non-positive integer (verify: echo '0' > .github/module-size-limit.txt && <run gate> → exit 1 with an 'invalid limit' message; echo 'abc' > .github/module-size-limit.txt → same)
```

findings.finding: What item 2a specifies the gate must 'fail if the file is missing OR not a positive int', but no AC tests the shell gate's runtime behavior when the limit file contains a non-positive-integer value (e.g., '0', '-1', 'foo'). AC6 (pytest) verifies the file content at commit time is a positive int, but does not test the gate script's error-handling path for a malformed file.
findings.location: What item 2a; AC4 (fourth bullet); AC6 (sixth bullet)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ACs: 'The gate fails when the limit file is non-positive-integer (verify: replace .github/module-size-limit.txt contents with "0" / "abc", run gate step → exit 1 with a parse-error message).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either update the parent epic (716f) to formally recognise the 2a/2b split and add Phase 2b as a named child story — or consolidate everything into this leaf so the parent's single-Phase-2 decomposition is satisfied.
evidence: Parent decomposition: 'Phase 2 — one child story: delete the allowlist+ceilings mechanism, replace the contract test with an absolute "nothing over 800" test, prune the architecture.md offenders table, and add the limit-lock gate.'
Parent AC #2: 'The allowlist/ceilings mechanism (both files + the sync contract test + the shell gate's allowlist branch) is removed'
Leaf What item 5: 'KEEP the (now-empty) allowlist + ceilings files in this change (Phase 2b deletes them)'
Leaf Notes: 'Phase 2b (follow-up) deletes the orphaned allowlist/ceilings files, prunes the docs/architecture.md offenders table, and updates AGENTS.md — kept separate'
findings.finding: The leaf (Phase 2a) introduces a two-part split (2a + 2b) that is not present in the parent epic's decomposition. The parent explicitly describes Phase 2 as 'one child story' covering: delete the allowlist+ceilings mechanism, replace the contract test, prune docs/architecture.md offenders table, and add the limit-lock gate. The leaf defers deletion of allowlist/ceilings files, pruning of docs/architecture.md, and the AGENTS.md update to an unplanned Phase 2b — but the parent has no Phase 2b concept, meaning those deliverables have no parent-sanctioned home.
findings.location: Leaf 'What' item 5 / 'Notes' section; Parent epic 716f 'Decomposition' (Phase 2) and ACs #2–#3
scenarios: If Phase 2b is never filed or landed, the parent's AC #2 (both files removed) and AC #3 (documented) remain unmet, but the parent epic may be marked complete after 2a lands.
The parent has no ticket representing Phase 2b, leaving the deletion of allowlist/ceilings files, docs/architecture.md pruning, and AGENTS.md update in an untracked state.
findings.suggested_fix: File a Phase 2b child story under epic 716f (not as an informal follow-up) that explicitly covers: delete .github/module-size-allowlist.txt and .github/module-size-ceilings.txt, prune docs/architecture.md offenders table, update AGENTS.md. Update the parent epic's Decomposition to say 'Phase 2a: swap gate; Phase 2b: delete orphaned files + update docs' so both children are within the parent's declared scope.

findings.finding: The leaf contradicts parent AC2 ('The allowlist/ceilings mechanism … is removed') by explicitly keeping the allowlist and ceilings files (What item 5, Notes section), and it contradicts the parent's Phase 2 decomposition ('one child story') by splitting delivery into an unsanctioned 2a + 2b without updating the parent.
findings.location: Leaf: What item 5 / Notes section; Parent 716f: Phase 2 Decomposition, AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Add a Phase 2b ticket and update parent epic 716f's Decomposition section to read: 'Phase 2a — rewrite gate + tests, add limit-lock (files kept for CI ordering); Phase 2b — delete allowlist/ceilings files, prune docs/architecture.md, update AGENTS.md.' Adjust parent AC2 to be satisfied only after both 2a and 2b land, or mark AC2 as a Phase 2b deliverable.

findings.finding: The leaf splits the parent's 'Phase 2 — one child story' into Phase 2a + Phase 2b without updating the parent, and explicitly keeps files the parent AC2 says must be removed (allowlist/ceilings), contradicting the parent's stated decomposition and acceptance criteria.
findings.location: Leaf What §5 / Notes; Parent Decomposition 'Phase 2' and Parent AC2
findings.suggested_fix: In the parent epic (716f), revise the Decomposition to read: 'Phase 2a — swap the gate to absolute cap + limit-lock (keeps legacy files for CI ordering); Phase 2b — delete allowlist/ceilings files, prune architecture.md offender table, update AGENTS.md.' Update AC2 to reference Phase 2b as the completion point. Then re-review the parent, after which this leaf is a clean subset.

findings.finding: The leaf defers the docs/architecture.md offenders-table pruning and AGENTS.md update to Phase 2b, contradicting parent AC3 ('documented') and the parent's file_impact entry for docs/architecture.md — so after Phase 2a lands, the 'documented' requirement of parent AC3 is unsatisfied.
findings.location: Leaf: Notes section ('prunes the docs/architecture.md offenders table, and updates AGENTS.md — kept separate'); Parent 716f: AC3, file_impact docs/architecture.md

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).

verify command `for f in .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml .github/workflows/test.yml; do grep -q 'module-size-limit.txt' "$f" || exit 1; ! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' "$f" || exit 1; ! grep -q 'module-size-ceilings' "$f" || exit 1; done && echo OK` has an unquoted shell variable ($VAR/$?) in its grep — it expands before grep runs; the pattern is not what you wrote

verify command `for f in .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml .github/workflows/test.yml; do grep -q 'must override' "$f" || exit 1; done && echo OK` prints OK, exit 0)` has an unquoted shell variable ($VAR/$?) in its grep — it expands before grep runs; the pattern is not what you wrote

verify command `for f in .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml .github/workflows/test.yml; do grep -q 'must override' "$f" || exit 1; done && echo OK` has an unquoted shell variable ($VAR/$?) in its grep — it expands before grep runs; the pattern is not what you wrote
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: What item 3 says to 'drop the allowlist/ceilings sync assertions' from `test_module_size_contract.py`, but AC bullet 4 only verifies that the test *passes* (`pytest … -q`). Since the legacy files are kept (non-empty content is irrelevant but they exist), old sync assertions might still pass if the files are present — a passing run does not confirm the old assertions were removed.
findings.location: What item 3 / AC bullet 4
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC bullet 4's verify: `&& ! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist\|module-size-ceilings' tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Provide concrete runnable shell snippets for AC 4 and AC 5 — e.g. for AC 4: `(cd /tmp && mkdir fake && bash .github/gate.sh 2>&1 || true) | grep 'must not be removed'`; for AC 5: inline the exact lock comparison snippet with `BASE=800 LIMIT=900 bash -c '<snippet>' && echo FAIL || echo OK`.
evidence: AC 4: 'verify: run the gate step against a tree with no `.github/module-size-limit.txt` → exit 1 with a 'must not be removed' message' — no concrete shell invocation given.
AC 5: 'verify: the lock snippet with BASE=800 LIMIT=900 → exit 1 with the override message; BASE=800 LIMIT=800 → exit 0' — describes a result without a runnable command.
Notes: 'the gate is shell (oracle = running it against crafted trees, per the ACs)' acknowledges the oracle but defers the exact invocation to the executor.
findings.finding: ACs 4 and 5 describe expected gate behavior in prose rather than providing concrete runnable shell commands, leaving the proving step underspecified for an executing agent.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 4 ('run the gate step against a tree with no .github/module-size-limit.txt') and AC 5 ('run the lock snippet with BASE=800 LIMIT=900')
scenarios: An executor implements the gate but runs a different invocation pattern than intended, passing AC 4/5 superficially without exercising the real guard.
A reviewer cannot independently reproduce the verification step from the plan text alone.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in Step 3 whether `test_compute_over_cap_is_deterministic` is kept (and how its `@pytest.mark.parametrize('cap', [SOFT_CAP])` is updated to use the file-sourced cap) or intentionally dropped with rationale.
evidence: Plan Step 3: 'Replace tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py: drop the allowlist/ceilings sync assertions; read the cap from the limit file; assert no src/rebar file exceeds it and the file parses to a positive int.'
Existing test_module_size_contract.py line 149: `def test_compute_over_cap_is_deterministic(cap: int) -> None:` — a parametrized pure-function test with no allowlist/ceilings dependency
Plan's replacement spec covers 2 new assertions but is silent on this 5th test
findings.finding: The plan says 'Replace `tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py`' but does not specify the disposition of `test_compute_over_cap_is_deterministic` — the one existing test function that has no dependency on `ALLOWLIST` or `CEILINGS` and is a pure-function correctness check on `compute_over_cap_modules`. The plan's replacement spec ('assert no src/rebar file exceeds it and the file parses to a positive int') does not mention this test, leaving an implementer to guess whether to keep or silently drop it.
findings.location: What section, Step 3 / `tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py`
scenarios: An implementer doing a clean 'replace' of the file drops `test_compute_over_cap_is_deterministic`, silently removing a determinism guard on the core computation function.

An implementer keeps the function but its `@pytest.mark.parametrize('cap', [SOFT_CAP])` references the old `SOFT_CAP` constant — now replaced by a file-read — requiring a parametrize update that the plan doesn't mention.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Step 3: 'Retain `test_compute_over_cap_is_deterministic` but update its parametrize value from the hardcoded `SOFT_CAP` constant to the file-sourced integer (e.g., read once at module level from `.github/module-size-limit.txt`).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact new `_SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES` entry (both key label and value) that replaces `'module-size ratchet': 'module-size-ceilings.txt'` in Step 4, e.g. `'module-size absolute cap': 'module-size-limit.txt'`.
evidence: Plan Step 4: 'Update tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py _SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES to a signature the NEW gate contains in both workflows (.github/module-size-limit.txt)'
Current code at test_ci_workflow_parity.py:50: `'module-size ratchet': 'module-size-ceilings.txt'` — key says 'ratchet', value will be stale after gate rewrite
Plan does not specify whether the dict key label ('module-size ratchet') is also renamed to reflect the new 'absolute cap' semantics
findings.finding: The plan says to update `_SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES` in `test_ci_workflow_parity.py` to a signature the new gate contains, but it does not name the exact dict key/value replacement. The current entry is `'module-size ratchet': 'module-size-ceilings.txt'` — the key label 'ratchet' is semantically incorrect for the new absolute-cap mechanism, and the value `module-size-ceilings.txt` will be absent from both workflow files after the rewrite. The plan's description ('a signature the NEW gate contains in both workflows (`.github/module-size-limit.txt`)') implies replacing the value only, but leaves the key rename ambiguous. An implementer who updates only the value but omits updating `test_no_drift_script_gate_is_verified_only_in_branch_ci` (which is unaffected) may not realize the key label is now misleading.
findings.location: What section, Step 4 / `tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py` `_SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES`
scenarios: An implementer swaps the value to `module-size-limit.txt` but leaves the key as `'module-size ratchet'`, resulting in a misleading label in the parity test that future contributors will need to decode.
A reviewer checking Step 4 completion cannot verify correctness without knowing the intended new key label.
findings.suggested_fix: In Step 4, write: "Replace the existing `'module-size ratchet': 'module-size-ceilings.txt'` entry with `'module-size absolute cap': 'module-size-limit.txt'`" to unambiguously specify both the key rename and value swap.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies the allowlist and ceilings files still exist in the tree (e.g., `test -f .github/module-size-allowlist.txt && test -f .github/module-size-ceilings.txt && echo OK`).
evidence: 'KEEP the (now-empty) allowlist + ceilings files in this change (Phase 2b deletes them), so main's current gate stays green on THIS change's CI.'
ABSENCE: no AC reads or asserts the presence of the allowlist/ceilings files
findings.finding: The deliverable 'KEEP the (now-empty) allowlist + ceilings files in this change' (What item 5) has no covering acceptance criterion — no AC verifies that these files are still present in the change's tree.
findings.location: What, item 5; Acceptance Criteria section (all ACs)
scenarios: An implementer deletes the files as part of the rewrite and has no failing AC to catch the mistake; main's CI run on the change then breaks due to the missing files read by the old gate
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '- [ ] The (now-empty) allowlist and ceilings files are still present (verify: `test -f .github/module-size-allowlist.txt && test -f .github/module-size-ceilings.txt && echo OK` prints OK, exit 0)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan to the Notes (e.g., 'If the new gate is defective, revert this commit on main via a fast-track admin push; the old gate is restored because the allowlist/ceilings files are still present until Phase 2b').
evidence: 'This is Phase 2a (the enforcement SWAP).'
ABSENCE: no section describes how to revert if the new gate breaks CI for legitimate changes after merge
T4 rubric: 'acknowledgement alone is insufficient; require the reversibility mechanism'
findings.finding: The plan performs a breaking change to the CI gate (swapping the allowlist-based mechanism for an absolute cap) but states no rollback or back-out plan if the new gate is defective after landing on main.
findings.location: Why section; Notes section
scenarios: The new gate has a shell bug (e.g., wrong glob) that rejects all changes; without a stated rollback plan, the team must discover the revert procedure ad hoc under incident pressure
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Notes: 'Rollback: if the new gate causes main-branch breakage, `git revert <this commit>` restores the allowlist-based gate (the allowlist/ceilings files are still present until Phase 2b, so the old gate is immediately functional again).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add AGENTS.md and/or docs/architecture.md updates to Phase 2a (not deferred to 2b) describing the new limit-file pattern, limit-lock mechanism, and admin-override requirement — or explicitly justify in the plan why these doc updates must wait for Phase 2b.
evidence: Notes: 'Phase 2b (follow-up) deletes the orphaned allowlist/ceilings files, prunes the docs/architecture.md offenders table, and updates AGENTS.md'
Phase 2a What items 1-5 contain no AGENTS.md or design-doc update
The plan justifies keeping allowlist/ceilings files for CI-sequencing reasons, but provides no equivalent justification for deferring AGENTS.md updates
New 'limit lock / admin override' pattern is a novel contribution-process decision not captured anywhere in this change
findings.finding: Phase 2a introduces a new architectural/process mechanism (admin-locked limit file + limit-lock CI gate with 'must override' enforcement) but defers all documentation updates (AGENTS.md, docs/architecture.md) to Phase 2b without a technical justification for the deferral. Unlike file deletion (which has a clear CI-sequencing reason), there is no reason AGENTS.md and architecture docs cannot be updated in this same change. After Phase 2a lands on main, AGENTS.md still describes the old allowlist/grandfathering contribution process, misleading contributors about how module-size enforcement works.
findings.location: Notes section: 'Phase 2b... updates AGENTS.md'; What section item 5: no doc update mentioned
scenarios: A contributor reads AGENTS.md after Phase 2a lands and follows the (now-defunct) allowlist/grandfathering process to request a module-size exemption
A future reviewer cannot find where the 'admin-locked limit' decision was documented, slowing onboarding or audit
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, add item 6: 'Update AGENTS.md: replace allowlist/grandfathering instructions with the new limit-file and admin-lock contribution rules. Update docs/architecture.md to reference .github/module-size-limit.txt as the single source of truth for the module-size cap.' Since AGENTS.md edits carry no CI-sequencing risk, they can and should land in this change.

findings.finding: The plan's What item 2e states 'The step no longer reads `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` or `.github/module-size-ceilings.txt` (in EITHER workflow)', but AC2's verification command only checks for removal of `module-size-allowlist` reads (`! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist'…`) from both workflow files — there is no corresponding check that `module-size-ceilings.txt` reads are also removed. This means the ceilings-file read (currently `ceilings=.github/module-size-ceilings.txt` in both workflows) could survive in the rewritten gate without any AC catching it.
findings.location: What item 2e; Acceptance Criteria bullet 2
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the AC2 bullet's verify block to: `! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml && ! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' .github/workflows/test.yml && ! grep -q 'module-size-ceilings' .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml && ! grep -q 'module-size-ceilings' .github/workflows/test.yml`

findings.finding: What item 2e states the gate 'no longer reads .github/module-size-ceilings.txt (in EITHER workflow)', but AC2's verify command only greps for 'module-size-allowlist' removal — there is no acceptance criterion that verifies the ceilings-file read is also removed from both workflows.
findings.location: What item 2e; AC2 (second bullet)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC2's verify line: append `&& ! grep -q 'module-size-ceilings' .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml && ! grep -q 'module-size-ceilings' .github/workflows/test.yml`

findings.finding: AC2's `grep -c 'module-size-limit.txt' file1 file2` command returns exit 0 if the pattern appears in ANY of the listed files, not necessarily BOTH — so the gate could be present in only one workflow and the AC would still appear to pass, silently missing the other file.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 (`grep -c 'module-size-limit.txt' .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml .github/workflows/test.yml`)
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace AC2's grep command with two independent checks:
```
grep -q 'module-size-limit.txt' .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml \
  && grep -q 'module-size-limit.txt' .github/workflows/test.yml
```
This asserts the pattern is present in EACH file and exits non-zero if either is missing.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
